entries.content: Unemployment rate series constructed from BEA Long Term Economic Growth (1966, Series B1-B2) for 1860-1970 and Economic Report of the President for 1948-2010. The two series are spliced to form a continuous annual unemployment rate. Both sources report civilian unemployment as a percentage of the labor force. S009A = LTEG, S009B = ERP; final S009 is the spliced series.

U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (1966). Long Term Economic Growth, 1860-1970. Series B1-B2. Economic Report of the President. Annual volumes 1948-2010. Both sources report unemployment rate (percent of civilian labor force).

Fig 2.7 displays US unemployment from 1890 to 2010. The series shows major cyclical peaks (1890s depression, 1930s Great Depression, 1980s recessions). FRED UNRATE provides direct continuation post-2010 - same underlying BLS series, identical units (percent). No reindexing required; direct splice at 2010.

citations.full: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (1966). Long Term Economic Growth, 1860-1970. Series B1-B2. Washington, DC: U.S. Government Printing Office.
Council of Economic Advisers. Economic Report of the President. Annual. Washington, DC: U.S. Government Printing Office. 1948-2010.
Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED). UNRATE - Unemployment Rate. Percent, seasonally adjusted. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/UNRATE

methodology_summary: S009 combines BEA LTEG unemployment (Series B1-B2, 1860-1970) with Economic Report of the President data (1948-2010). Splicing produces continuous annual civilian unemployment rate (percent) from 1890-2010. FRED UNRATE provides direct continuation - identical BLS-derived series, same units. No transformation needed at splice; extension is seamless.
known_issues: LTEG coverage 1860-1970 vs ERP 1948-2010 - overlap period uses ERP (more recent source)
Pre-1948 unemployment estimates from LTEG may differ from current BLS methodology

cross_references.relationship: Labor market context - unemployment affects real compensation trends
Direct continuation - same series, identical units
